title: Pair-Breaking and Dimensionality in Spin-Orbit Coupled Superconductors
authors: Reiley Dorrian, Mizuki Ohno, Elena Williams, Adrian Llanos, Joseph Falson
abstract: The response of ultra-thin superconducting materials under parallel magnetic fields is often leveraged to obtain insight into the nature of the condensate, including features attributable to unconventional forms of pairing. Despite there being multiple competing mechanisms responsible for suppressing superconductivity, it is common for these analyses to overlook certain depairing channels. Here we report an analysis of thickness dependent superconductivity in thin films of \ce{LaBi2} using the multi-mechanism Kharitonov-Feigel'man framework . By resolving field-enhanced superconductivity in the thin-limit, we obtain an estimate the role of spin exchange scattering, in addition to paramagnetic and orbital effects. Our analyses offer insight into how fundamental quantities such as the critical temperature as well as Pauli limit are defined, recasting the landscape for how scattering times in two-dimensional superconductors can be interpreted.
summary: This paper investigates how different depairing mechanisms like orbital and magnetic scattering affect superconductivity in ultra-thin LaBi2 films. It demonstrates that using a more comprehensive model (KF) is essential to avoid significant errors in estimating spin-orbit scattering rates. The work provides a more accurate framework for interpreting the physics of two-dimensional, spin-orbit coupled superconductors.
main_problem: Evaluating the accuracy of depairing models (KLB vs. KF) in describing superconductivity in ultra-thin, spin-orbit coupled films and understanding the mechanisms (orbital, paramagnetic, and magnetic) that suppress the superconducting state.
main_result: The KF model more accurately captures individual scattering channels and resolves a systematic artifact in the KLB model, which overestimates spin-orbit scattering time by neglecting orbital and magnetic effects. The study also identifies field-enhanced superconductivity in the ultra-thin limit due to the suppression of magnetic fluctuations.
method: The researchers synthesized LaBi2 thin films via molecular beam epitaxy and performed low-temperature transport measurements in a vector magnet. They compared the KLB and KF theoretical frameworks by fitting temperature-dependent upper critical fields and using Drude analysis to estimate carrier properties.
model_or_system: The physical system consists of ultra-thin LaBi2 films (2.1 nm to 75 nm) with significant spin-orbit coupling. The study utilizes the multi-mechanism Kharitonov-Feigel'man (KF) model and the Klemm-Luther-Beasley (KLB) model to account for orbital pair-breaking, paramagnetic effects, and magnetic scattering.
key_observables: Zero-field critical temperature (Tc), in-plane upper critical field (Hc2), resistance transitions (R/R0), magnetoresistance, and Hall resistivity.
important_parameters: Thickness (t), spin-orbit scattering time (tau_SO), transport scattering time (tau_tr), magnetic scattering rate (nu_s), and the Pauli limit violation ratio.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes a dirty-limit regime (xi/l >> 1) and uses a free-electron approximation for estimating Fermi momentum, which may be limited by the actual effective mass in LaBi2.
figures_summary: XRD patterns and Laue fringe analysis for thickness estimation; normalized zero-field resistance transitions; comparison of KF and KLB model fits; and plots showing the sensitivity of scattering parameters to different fitting constraints.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of depairing mechanisms, describes the synthesis and characterization of LaBi2 films, presents transport measurement results, compares the KLB and KF models through fitting analysis, and discusses the implications for interpreting scattering rates in 2D superconductors.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
